column4: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
column5: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
column6: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided.
column7: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , and that file sets TLSCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set TLSCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher-suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher-suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.
column14: By default the Kubernetes API server supports a wide range of TLS ciphers
column15: The list chosen above should be fine for modern clients. It's essentially the list from the Mozilla "Modern cipher" option with the ciphersuites supporting CBC mode removed, as CBC has traditionally had a lot of issues
column19: The Kubelet configuration for TLS cipher suites is typically managed at the node level, often through systemd service files or configuration files on the nodes themselves, which are not directly accessible via Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl`. While some information can be gathered using `kubectl`, the specific configuration of TLS cipher suites requires node-level access, which involves manual inspection or SSH access to the nodes.
column21: 1. SSH into each node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. Run `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to check for the presence of the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument in the Kubelet command line. 3. If the `--tls-cipher-suites` argument is present, verify that it only includes the following secure ciphers: - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 4. If using a Kubelet configuration file, locate the file specified by the `--config` argument and check the `TLSCipherSuites` setting to ensure it matches the secure ciphers listed above. 5. Confirm compliance by ensuring no insecure ciphers are present in the configuration.

column4: Ensure that the cluster-admin role is only used where required
column5: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a set of default roles where RBAC is used. Some of these roles such as cluster-admin provide wide-ranging privileges which should only be applied where absolutely necessary. Roles such as cluster-admin allow super-user access to perform any action on any resource. When used in a ClusterRoleBinding , it gives full control over every resource in the cluster and in all namespaces. When used in a RoleBinding , it gives full control over every resource in the rolebinding's namespace, including the namespace itself.
column7: Obtain a list of the principals who have access to the cluster-admin role by reviewing the clusterrolebinding output for each role binding that has access to the cluster- admin role. kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name Review each principal listed and ensure that cluster-admin privilege is required for it.
column8: Identify all clusterrolebindings to the cluster-admin role. Check if they are used and if they need this role or if they could use a role with fewer privileges. Where possible, first bind users to a lower privileged role and then remove the clusterrolebinding to the cluster-admin role : kubectl delete clusterrolebinding [name]
column10: Care should be taken before removing any clusterrolebindings from the environment to ensure they were not required for operation of the cluster. Specifically, modifications should not be made to clusterrolebindings with the system: prefix as they are required for the operation of system components.
column14: By default a single clusterrolebinding called cluster-admin is provided with the system:masters group as its principal.
column19: The task of identifying which principals have access to the `cluster-admin` role can be effectively automated using the `kubectl` command to query `clusterrolebindings`. This command provides a comprehensive list of bindings and their associated subjects, allowing for an automated check of role assignments. While human judgment is required to assess the necessity of these assignments, the initial data retrieval process is fully automatable using Kubernetes CLI capabilities.

column4: Minimize access to secrets
column5: The Kubernetes API stores secrets, which may be service account tokens for the Kubernetes API or credentials used by workloads in the cluster. Access to these secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
column6: Inappropriate access to secrets stored within the Kubernetes cluster can allow for an attacker to gain additional access to the Kubernetes cluster or external resources whose credentials are stored as secrets.
column7: Review the users who have get , list or watch access to secrets objects in the Kubernetes API.
column8: Where possible, remove get , list and watch access to secret objects in the cluster.

column4: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
column5: Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles provide access to resources based on sets of objects and actions that can be taken on those objects. It is possible to set either of these to be the wildcard "*" which matches all items. Use of wildcards is not optimal from a security perspective as it may allow for inadvertent access to be granted when new resources are added to the Kubernetes API either as CRDs or in later versions of the product.
column6: The principle of least privilege recommends that users are provided only the access required for their role and nothing more. The use of wildcard rights grants is likely to provide excessive rights to the Kubernetes API.
column7: Retrieve the roles defined across each namespaces in the cluster and review for wildcards kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml Retrieve the cluster roles defined in the cluster and review for wildcards kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml
column8: Where possible replace any use of wildcards in clusterroles and roles with specific objects or actions.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that the audit approach should be automated, and I concur with this assessment. The use of kubectl commands to retrieve and evaluate Roles and ClusterRoles for wildcard entries is straightforward and can be effectively executed using Kubernetes APIs or CLI. The criteria for evaluation are clear and objective, making automation feasible and efficient.
column20: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Roles and ClusterRoles - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml` and `kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml` - Properties to Check: `rules` section for `verbs`, `resources`, and `resourceNames` containing `*` - Pass Criteria: No Roles or ClusterRoles contain wildcards in the specified fields. - Fail Criteria: At least one Role or ClusterRole contains wildcards in the specified fields.

column4: Minimize access to create pods
column5: The ability to create pods in a namespace can provide a number of opportunities for privilege escalation, such as assigning privileged service accounts to these pods or mounting hostPaths with access to sensitive data (unless Pod Security Policies are implemented to restrict this access) As such, access to create new pods should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users.
column6: The ability to create pods in a cluster opens up possibilities for privilege escalation and should be restricted, where possible.
column7: Review the users who have create access to pod objects in the Kubernetes API.
column8: Where possible, remove create access to pod objects in the cluster.
column10: Care should be taken not to remove access to pods to system components which require this for their operation

column4: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
column5: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a default service account which is used by cluster workloads where no specific service account is assigned to the pod. Where access to the Kubernetes API from a pod is required, a specific service account should be created for that pod, and rights granted to that service account. The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
column7: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
column8: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false
column10: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
column14: By default the default service account allows for its service account token to be mounted in pods in its namespace.
column19: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes CLI commands to check the default service accounts across all namespaces. The commands can be automated to verify the absence of RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings for default service accounts and ensure the `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false`. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion providing a more comprehensive command for checking all namespaces.
column20: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** ServiceAccounts - **API/CLI Method:** - List all service accounts: `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces` - Check RoleBindings: `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name == "default" and .subjects[]?.kind == "ServiceAccount")'` - Check ClusterRoleBindings: `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name == "default" and .subjects[]?.kind == "ServiceAccount")'` - Verify `automountServiceAccountToken`: `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.namespace}:{@.metadata.name}={@.automountServiceAccountToken}{"\n"}{end}' | grep ":default="` - **Properties to Check:** - RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings for default service accounts - `automountServiceAccountToken` field for default service accounts - **Pass Criteria:** No RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings found for default service accounts, and `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false`. - **Fail Criteria:** Any RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings found for default service accounts, or `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `true`.
column27: The compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes CLI commands to check the default service accounts across all namespaces. column28: - **Kubernetes Resource/Service:** ServiceAccounts - **API/CLI Method:** - List all service accounts: `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces` - Check RoleBindings: `kubectl get rolebindings --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name == "default" and .subjects[]?.kind == "ServiceAccount")'` - Check ClusterRoleBindings: `kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.subjects[]?.name == "default" and .subjects[]?.kind == "ServiceAccount")'` - Verify `automountServiceAccountToken`: `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.namespace}:{@.metadata.name}={@.automountServiceAccountToken}{"\n"}{end}' | grep ":default="` - **Properties to Check:** - RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings for default service accounts - `automountServiceAccountToken` field for default service accounts - **Pass Criteria:** No RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings found for default service accounts, and `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `false`. - **Fail Criteria:** Any RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings found for default service accounts, or `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to `true`.

column4: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted where necessary
column5: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
column6: Mounting service account tokens inside pods can provide an avenue for privilege escalation attacks where an attacker is able to compromise a single pod in the cluster. Avoiding mounting these tokens removes this attack avenue.
column7: Review pod and service account objects in the cluster and ensure that the option below is set, unless the resource explicitly requires this access. automountServiceAccountToken: false
column8: Modify the definition of pods and service accounts which do not need to mount service account tokens to disable it.
column10: Pods mounted without service account tokens will not be able to communicate with the API server, except where the resource is available to unauthenticated principals.
column14: By default, all pods get a service account token mounted in them.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that this compliance control can be effectively audited using Kubernetes API objects by checking the `automountServiceAccountToken` field in Pod and ServiceAccount definitions. column4: Avoid use of system:masters group
column5: The special group system:masters should not be used to grant permissions to any user or service account, except where strictly necessary (e.g. bootstrapping access prior to RBAC being fully available)
column6: The system:masters group has unrestricted access to the Kubernetes API hard-coded into the API server source code. An authenticated user who is a member of this group cannot have their access reduced, even if all bindings and cluster role bindings which mention it, are removed. When combined with client certificate authentication, use of this group can allow for irrevocable cluster-admin level credentials to exist for a cluster.
column7: Review a list of all credentials which have access to the cluster and ensure that the group system:masters is not used.
column8: Remove the system:masters group from all users in the cluster.
column10: Once the RBAC system is operational in a cluster system:masters should not be specifically required, as ordinary bindings from principals to the cluster-admin cluster role can be made where unrestricted access is required.
column14: By default some clusters will create a "break glass" client certificate which is a member of this group. Access to this client certificate should be carefully controlled and it should not be used for general cluster operations.

column4: Limit use of the Bind, Impersonate and Escalate permissions in the Kubernetes cluster
column5: Cluster roles and roles with the impersonate, bind or escalate permissions should not be granted unless strictly required. Each of these permissions allow a particular subject to escalate their privileges beyond those explicitly granted by cluster administrators
column6: The impersonate privilege allows a subject to impersonate other users gaining their rights to the cluster. The bind privilege allows the subject to add a binding to a cluster role or role which escalates their effective permissions in the cluster. The escalate privilege allows a subject to modify cluster roles to which they are bound, increasing their rights to that level. Each of these permissions has the potential to allow for privilege escalation to cluster-admin level.
column7: Review the users who have access to cluster roles or roles which provide the impersonate, bind or escalate privileges.
column8: Where possible, remove the impersonate, bind and escalate rights from subjects.
column10: There are some cases where these permissions are required for cluster service operation, and care should be taken before removing these permissions from system service accounts.
column14: In a default kubeadm cluster, the system:masters group and clusterrole-aggregation-controller service account have access to the escalate privilege. The system:masters group also has access to bind and impersonate.
column19: The control focuses on reviewing RBAC permissions related to impersonate, bind, and escalate, which can be effectively queried using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. column4: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
column5: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true .
column6: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one PodSecurityPolicy (PSP) defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate PSP and you should carefully check RBAC controls to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to access that PSP.

column4: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
column5: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
column6: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace.
column7: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl --all-namespaces get networkpolicy Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.

column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. column4: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
column5: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
column6: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default . You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users.

column6: This admission controller rejects all net-new usage of the Service field externalIPs. This feature is very powerful (allows network traffic interception) and not well controlled by policy. When enabled, users of the cluster may not create new Services which use externalIPs and may not add new values to externalIPs on existing Service objects. Existing uses of externalIPs are not affected, and users may remove values from externalIPs on existing Service objects. Most users do not need this feature at all, and cluster admins should consider disabling it. Clusters that do need to use this feature should consider using some custom policy to manage usage of it.

column4: Ensure that the admission control plugin AlwaysPullImages is set
column5: Always pull images.
column6: Setting admission control policy to AlwaysPullImages forces every new pod to pull the required images every time. In a multi-tenant cluster users can be assured that their private images can only be used by those who have the credentials to pull them. Without this admission control policy, once an image has been pulled to a node, any pod from any user can use it simply by knowing the image’s name, without any authorization check against the image ownership. When this plug-in is enabled, images are always pulled prior to starting containers, which means valid credentials are required.

This setting is not appropriate for clusters which use this configuration.

column5: Use individual service account credentials for each controller.

column4: Client certificate authentication should not be used for users
column5: Kubernetes provides the option to use client certificates for user authentication. However as there is no way to revoke these certificates when a user leaves an organization or loses their credential, they are not suitable for this purpose. It is not possible to fully disable client certificate use within a cluster as it is used for component to component authentication.
column6: With any authentication mechanism the ability to revoke credentials if they are compromised or no longer required, is a key control. Kubernetes client certificate authentication does not allow for this due to a lack of support for certificate revocation.
column7: Review user access to the cluster and ensure that users are not making use of Kubernetes client certificate authentication.
column8: Alternative mechanisms provided by Kubernetes such as the use of OIDC should be implemented in place of client certificates.
column10: External mechanisms for authentication generally require additional software to be deployed.
column14: Client certificate authentication is enabled by default.
column15: The lack of certificate revocation was flagged up as a high risk issue in the recent Kubernetes security audit. Without this feature, client certificate authentication is not suitable for end users.
column19: The control requires verifying user authentication methods, specifically ensuring that client certificate authentication is not used for users. This involves reviewing the `kube-apiserver` configuration, which is not directly accessible via Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools like `kubectl`. The need to manually inspect server-side configurations or command-line arguments makes this a manual process. The first AI opinion correctly identifies the manual nature of this task, while the second AI suggests partial automation, which is not feasible given the current Kubernetes API capabilities.

Instead, verify that alternative authentication methods such as OIDC are in place. 4. **Confirm Compliance:** - Document any instances where client certificate authentication is found for users and recommend transitioning to a more secure authentication method, such as OIDC.

column7: Review the audit policy provided for the cluster and ensure that it covers at least the following areas :- • Access to Secrets managed by the cluster. Care should be taken to only log Metadata for requests to Secrets, ConfigMaps, and TokenReviews, in order to avoid the risk of logging sensitive data. • Modification of pod and deployment objects. • Use of pods/exec , pods/portforward , pods/proxy and services/proxy . For most requests, minimally logging at the Metadata level is recommended (the most basic level of logging).

column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

column4: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set to true
column5: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
column6: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that if the --make-iptables-util-chains argument exists then it is set to true . If the --make-iptables-util-chains argument does not exist, and there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , verify that the file does not set makeIPTablesUtilChains to false .
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set makeIPTablesUtilChains: true . If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove the --make- iptables-util-chains argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Kubelet would manage the iptables on the system and keep it in sync. If you are using any other iptables management solution, then there might be some conflicts.

column4: Ensure that the --hostname-override argument is not set
column5: Do not override node hostnames.
column6: Overriding hostnames could potentially break TLS setup between the kubelet and the apiserver. Additionally, with overridden hostnames, it becomes increasingly difficult to associate logs with a particular node and process them for security analytics. Hence, you should setup your kubelet nodes with resolvable FQDNs and avoid overriding the hostnames with IPs.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that --hostname-override argument does not exist. Note This setting is not configurable via the Kubelet config file.
column8: Edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and remove the --hostname-override argument from the KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Some cloud providers may require this flag to ensure that hostname matches names issued by the cloud provider. In these environments, this recommendation should not apply.
column14: By default, --hostname-override argument is not set.
column19: The compliance control requires checking the presence of the `--hostname-override` argument in the kubelet process, which is not directly exposed through Kubernetes APIs or CLI tools like `kubectl`. The task involves inspecting the process arguments on each node, which necessitates SSH access to the nodes. While some aspects could potentially be scripted, the core requirement of examining process arguments remains a manual task due to the lack of direct API support for this specific check.
column21: 1. SSH into each worker node where the kubelet is running. 2. Execute the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to list the kubelet process and its arguments. 3. Verify that the `--hostname-override` argument is not present in the output. 4. If the `--hostname-override` argument is found, document its presence as a compliance issue. 5. To remediate, edit the kubelet service file located at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf` and remove the `--hostname-override` argument from the `KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS` variable. 6. After making changes, run the commands: `systemctl daemon-reload` and `systemctl restart kubelet.service` to apply the changes. 7. Re-run the command from step 2 to confirm that the `--hostname-override` argument has been removed.

column4: Ensure that the --event-qps argument is set to 0 or a level which ensures appropriate event capture
column5: Security relevant information should be captured. The --event-qps flag on the Kubelet can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
column6: It is important to capture all events and not restrict event creation. Events are an important source of security information and analytics that ensure that your environment is consistently monitored using the event data.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Review the value set for the --event-qps argument and determine whether this has been set to an appropriate level for the cluster. The value of 0 can be used to ensure that all events are captured. If the --event-qps argument does not exist, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config and review the value in this location.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set eventRecordQPS: to an appropriate level. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf on each worker node and set the below parameter in KUBELET_SYSTEM_PODS_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Setting this parameter to 0 could result in a denial of service condition due to excessive events being created. The cluster's event processing and storage systems should be scaled to handle expected event loads.
column14: By default, --event-qps argument is set to 5 .
column19: The compliance control requires checking the `--event-qps` argument on the Kubelet, which is typically configured at the node level and not exposed through Kubernetes APIs or ConfigMaps. The Kubelet runs as a system service on nodes, not as a pod, making it necessary to manually inspect the node's configuration or service files. The automation suggested by the first AI opinion is not feasible since Kubelet configurations are not typically managed through Kubernetes resources accessible via kubectl.
column21: 1. SSH into each node in the Kubernetes cluster. 2. Run the command `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to identify the current command-line arguments for the Kubelet service. 3. Check for the `--event-qps` argument in the output. If it is not present, locate the Kubelet configuration file specified by the `--config` flag. 4. Open the Kubelet configuration file (e.g., `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf`) and verify the `eventRecordQPS` setting. 5. Ensure that the `eventRecordQPS` is set to `0` or a level that ensures appropriate event capture without causing a denial of service. 6. If changes are needed, edit the configuration file and restart the Kubelet service using `systemctl daemon-reload` and `systemctl restart kubelet.service`.

column4: Ensure that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments are set as appropriate
column5: Setup TLS connection on the Kubelets.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks.
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --tls-cert-file and --tls-private-key-file arguments exist and they are set as appropriate. If these arguments are not present, check that there is a Kubelet config specified by --config and that it contains appropriate settings for tlsCertFile and tlsPrivateKeyFile.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set tlsCertFile to the location of the certificate file to use to identify this Kubelet, and tlsPrivateKeyFile to the location of the corresponding private key file. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the below parameters in KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. --tls-cert-file=<path/to/tls-certificate-file> --tls-private-key-file=<path/to/tls-key-file> Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column19: The compliance control requires verifying the presence and correctness of the `--tls-cert-file` and `--tls-private-key-file` arguments in the Kubelet configuration. While some clusters may use a ConfigMap for Kubelet configuration, this is not universally applicable, and many environments manage these settings directly on the node. The audit step involves using `ps -ef | grep kubelet`, which requires host-level access and cannot be automated through Kubernetes APIs or kubectl alone.
column21: 1. Log into each node in the Kubernetes cluster where Kubelet is running. 2. Execute the command `ps -ef | grep kubelet` to list the running Kubelet process and its arguments. 3. If the arguments are not present, check if a Kubelet config file is specified using the `--config` argument. 5. Confirm that the certificate and key files exist at the specified paths and are correctly configured for secure communication.

column4: Ensure that the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to false
column5: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation.
column6: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there is no downtime due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Note: This feature also require the RotateKubeletClientCertificate feature gate to be enabled (which is the default since Kubernetes v1.7)
column7: Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet Verify that the --rotate-certificates argument is not present, or is set to true . If the --rotate-certificates argument is not present, verify that if there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , that file does not contain rotateCertificates: false .
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to add the line rotateCertificates: true or remove it altogether to use the default value. If using command line arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and remove --rotate- certificates=false argument from the KUBELET_CERTIFICATE_ARGS variable. Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column14: By default, kubelet client certificate rotation is enabled.
column19: The compliance control requires checking the presence and value of the `--rotate-certificates` argument in the kubelet command line or configuration file on each node. This involves accessing the node's host-level configurations, which cannot be fully automated using Kubernetes APIs or `kubectl` alone. The need to inspect the kubelet service file or command line arguments directly on the host indicates a manual process.

column4: Verify that the RotateKubeletServerCertificate argument is set to true
column5: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation.
column6: RotateKubeletServerCertificate causes the kubelet to both request a serving certificate after bootstrapping its client credentials and rotate the certificate as its existing credentials expire. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself.

column4: Ensure that the Kubelet only makes use of Strong Cryptographic Ciphers
column5: Ensure that the Kubelet is configured to only use strong cryptographic ciphers.
column6: TLS ciphers have had a number of known vulnerabilities and weaknesses, which can reduce the protection provided by them. By default Kubernetes supports a number of TLS ciphersuites including some that have security concerns, weakening the protection provided.
column7: The set of cryptographic ciphers currently considered secure is the following: • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305 • TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 • TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Run the following command on each node: ps -ef | grep kubelet If the --tls-cipher-suites argument is present, ensure it only contains values included in this set. If it is not present check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config , and that file sets TLSCipherSuites: to only include values from this set.
column8: If using a Kubelet config file, edit the file to set TLSCipherSuites: to TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 or to a subset of these values. If using executable arguments, edit the kubelet service file /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf on each worker node and set the --tls-cipher-suites parameter as follows, or to a subset of these values. --tls-cipher-suites=TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305,TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 Based on your system, restart the kubelet service. For example: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service
column10: Kubelet clients that cannot support modern cryptographic ciphers will not be able to make connections to the Kubelet API.

column4: Ensure that the cluster-admin role is only used where required
column5: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a set of default roles where RBAC is used. Some of these roles such as cluster-admin provide wide-ranging privileges which should only be applied where absolutely necessary. Roles such as cluster-admin allow super-user access to perform any action on any resource. When used in a ClusterRoleBinding , it gives full control over every resource in the cluster and in all namespaces. When used in a RoleBinding , it gives full control over every resource in the rolebinding's namespace, including the namespace itself.
column7: Obtain a list of the principals who have access to the cluster-admin role by reviewing the clusterrolebinding output for each role binding that has access to the cluster- admin role. kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name Review each principal listed and ensure that cluster-admin privilege is required for it.
column8: Identify all clusterrolebindings to the cluster-admin role. Check if they are used and if they need this role or if they could use a role with fewer privileges. Where possible, first bind users to a lower privileged role and then remove the clusterrolebinding to the cluster-admin role : kubectl delete clusterrolebinding [name]
column10: Care should be taken before removing any clusterrolebindings from the environment to ensure they were not required for operation of the cluster. Specifically, modifications should not be made to clusterrolebindings with the system: prefix as they are required for the operation of system components.
column14: By default a single clusterrolebinding called cluster-admin is provided with the system:masters group as its principal.
column19: The audit process for determining the usage of the `cluster-admin` role can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The necessary information about cluster role bindings and their associated subjects can be retrieved programmatically, allowing for an automated check of compliance. While manual review may be needed to assess the justification for each subject's access, the initial data gathering and identification of potential issues can be fully automated.

column4: Minimize access to secrets
column5: The Kubernetes API stores secrets, which may be service account tokens for the Kubernetes API or credentials used by workloads in the cluster. Access to these secrets should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users to reduce the risk of privilege escalation.
column6: Inappropriate access to secrets stored within the Kubernetes cluster can allow for an attacker to gain additional access to the Kubernetes cluster or external resources whose credentials are stored as secrets.
column7: Review the users who have get , list or watch access to secrets objects in the Kubernetes API.

column4: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
column5: Kubernetes Roles and ClusterRoles provide access to resources based on sets of objects and actions that can be taken on those objects. It is possible to set either of these to be the wildcard "*" which matches all items. Use of wildcards is not optimal from a security perspective as it may allow for inadvertent access to be granted when new resources are added to the Kubernetes API either as CRDs or in later versions of the product.
column6: The principle of least privilege recommends that users are provided only the access required for their role and nothing more. The use of wildcard rights grants is likely to provide excessive rights to the Kubernetes API.
column7: Retrieve the roles defined across each namespaces in the cluster and review for wildcards kubectl get roles --all-namespaces -o yaml Retrieve the cluster roles defined in the cluster and review for wildcards kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml
column8: Where possible replace any use of wildcards in clusterroles and roles with specific objects or actions.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that the control can be automated using Kubernetes CLI commands to retrieve and analyze Roles and ClusterRoles for wildcard usage. This approach leverages existing Kubernetes capabilities and does not require manual inspection, making it a suitable candidate for automation.

column4: Minimize access to create pods
column5: The ability to create pods in a namespace can provide a number of opportunities for privilege escalation, such as assigning privileged service accounts to these pods or mounting hostPaths with access to sensitive data (unless Pod Security Policies are implemented to restrict this access) As such, access to create new pods should be restricted to the smallest possible group of users.
column6: The ability to create pods in a cluster opens up possibilities for privilege escalation and should be restricted, where possible.

column4: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
column5: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a default service account which is used by cluster workloads where no specific service account is assigned to the pod. Where access to the Kubernetes API from a pod is required, a specific service account should be created for that pod, and rights granted to that service account. The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
column7: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
column8: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false
column10: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
column14: By default the default service account allows for its service account token to be mounted in pods in its namespace.

column4: Avoid use of system:masters group
column5: The special group system:masters should not be used to grant permissions to any user or service account, except where strictly necessary (e.g. bootstrapping access prior to RBAC being fully available)
column6: The system:masters group has unrestricted access to the Kubernetes API hard-coded into the API server source code. An authenticated user who is a member of this group cannot have their access reduced, even if all bindings and cluster role bindings which mention it, are removed. When combined with client certificate authentication, use of this group can allow for irrevocable cluster-admin level credentials to exist for a cluster.
column7: Review a list of all credentials which have access to the cluster and ensure that the group system:masters is not used.
column8: Remove the system:masters group from all users in the cluster.
column10: Once the RBAC system is operational in a cluster system:masters should not be specifically required, as ordinary bindings from principals to the cluster-admin cluster role can be made where unrestricted access is required.
column14: By default some clusters will create a "break glass" client certificate which is a member of this group. Access to this client certificate should be carefully controlled and it should not be used for general cluster operations.

column4: Limit use of the Bind, Impersonate and Escalate permissions in the Kubernetes cluster
column5: Cluster roles and roles with the impersonate, bind or escalate permissions should not be granted unless strictly required. Each of these permissions allow a particular subject to escalate their privileges beyond those explicitly granted by cluster administrators
column6: The impersonate privilege allows a subject to impersonate other users gaining their rights to the cluster. The bind privilege allows the subject to add a binding to a cluster role or role which escalates their effective permissions in the cluster. The escalate privilege allows a subject to modify cluster roles to which they are bound, increasing their rights to that level. Each of these permissions has the potential to allow for privilege escalation to cluster-admin level.
column7: Review the users who have access to cluster roles or roles which provide the impersonate, bind or escalate privileges.
column8: Where possible, remove the impersonate, bind and escalate rights from subjects.
column10: There are some cases where these permissions are required for cluster service operation, and care should be taken before removing these permissions from system service accounts.
column14: In a default kubeadm cluster, the system:masters group and clusterrole-aggregation-controller service account have access to the escalate privilege. The system:masters group also has access to bind and impersonate.

column4: Ensure that the cluster has at least one active policy control mechanism in place
column5: Every Kubernetes cluster should have at least one policy control mechanism in place to enforce the other requirements in this section. This could be the in-built Pod Security Admission controller, or a third party policy control system.
column6: Without an active policy control mechanism, it is not possible to limit the use of containers with access to underlying cluster nodes, via mechanisms like privileged containers, or the use of hostPath volume mounts.
column7: Pod Security Admission is enabled by default on all clusters using Kubernetes 1.23 or higher. To assess what controls, if any, are in place using this mechanism, review the namespaces in the cluster to see if the required labels have been applied kubectl get namespaces -o yaml To confirm if any external policy control system is in use, review the cluster for the presence of validatingadmissionwebhook and mutatingadmissionwebhook objects. kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations
column8: Ensure that either Pod Security Admission or an external policy control system is in place for every namespace which contains user workloads.
column10: Where policy control systems are in place, there is a risk that workloads required for the operation of the cluster may be stopped from running. Care is required when implementing admission control policies to ensure that this does not occur.
column14: By default, Pod Security Admission is enabled but no policies are in place.

column4: Minimize the admission of privileged containers
column5: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the securityContext.privileged flag set to true .
column6: Privileged containers have access to all Linux Kernel capabilities and devices. A container running with full privileges can do almost everything that the host can do. This flag exists to allow special use-cases, like manipulating the network stack and accessing devices. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit privileged containers. If you need to run privileged containers, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
column7: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of privileged containers.
column8: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of privileged containers.
column10: Pods defined with spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged: true , spec.initContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true and spec.ephemeralContainers[].securityContext.privileged: true will not be permitted.
column14: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of privileged containers.
column19: The control can be effectively automated using Kubernetes APIs and CLI tools to verify the presence of policies that restrict privileged containers. Both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of automation, with the second opinion highlighting the need to adapt to newer methods.

column4: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace
column5: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
column6: A container running in the host's PID namespace can inspect processes running outside the container. If the container also has access to ptrace capabilities this can be used to escalate privileges outside of the container. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to share the host PID namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostPID, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
column7: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of hostPID containers
column8: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of hostPID containers.
column10: Pods defined with spec.hostPID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
column14: By default, there are no restrictions on the creation of hostPID containers.

column4: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
column5: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
column6: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace.
column7: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl --all-namespaces get networkpolicy Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy.
column8: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them.
column10: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
column14: By default, network policies are not created.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Kubernetes APIs, specifically through the `kubectl` command to check for Network Policies across all namespaces. The second opinion suggests an improvement by ensuring that all namespaces are checked for the presence of at least one Network Policy, which can be done by listing all namespaces and cross-referencing them with those that have Network Policies. This task is feasible with Kubernetes CLI capabilities.
column20: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: NetworkPolicies - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get namespaces` and `kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces` - Properties to Check: Ensure each namespace has at least one NetworkPolicy defined. - Pass Criteria: Every namespace listed by `kubectl get namespaces` has at least one corresponding NetworkPolicy. - Fail Criteria: At least one namespace does not have any NetworkPolicy defined.

column4: Prefer using secrets as files over secrets as environment variables
column5: Kubernetes supports mounting secrets as data volumes or as environment variables. Minimize the use of environment variable secrets.
column6: It is reasonably common for application code to log out its environment (particularly in the event of an error). This will include any secret values passed in as environment variables, so secrets can easily be exposed to any user or entity who has access to the logs.
column7: Run the following command to find references to objects which use environment variables defined from secrets. kubectl get all -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@..secretKeyRef)]} {.kind} {.metadata.name} {"\n"}{end}' -A
column8: If possible, rewrite application code to read secrets from mounted secret files, rather than from environment variables.
column10: Application code which expects to read secrets in the form of environment variables would need modification
column14: By default, secrets are not defined
column15: Mounting secrets as volumes has the additional benefit that secret values can be updated without restarting the pod

column4: Consider external secret storage
column5: Consider the use of an external secrets storage and management system, instead of using Kubernetes Secrets directly, if you have more complex secret management needs. Ensure the solution requires authentication to access secrets, has auditing of access to and use of secrets, and encrypts secrets. Some solutions also make it easier to rotate secrets.
column6: Kubernetes supports secrets as first-class objects, but care needs to be taken to ensure that access to secrets is carefully limited. Using an external secrets provider can ease the management of access to secrets, especially where secrests are used across both Kubernetes and non-Kubernetes environments.
column7: Review your secrets management implementation.
column8: Refer to the secrets management options offered by your cloud provider or a third-party secrets management solution.
column14: By default, no external secret management is configured.
column19: While some aspects of secret management can be automated, such as identifying the use of Kubernetes Secrets with `kubectl`, the comprehensive evaluation of external secret management solutions requires manual inspection. This includes verifying authentication mechanisms, auditing capabilities, and encryption settings, which are typically configured outside of Kubernetes and require reviewing external systems' documentation and configurations.
column21: 1. Review the current secrets management implementation in your Kubernetes environment by checking the configurations of your applications and how they access secrets. 2. Verify if an external secrets management solution is in use by looking for integrations with tools like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. 3. Confirm that the external secrets management solution requires authentication to access secrets by reviewing the documentation of the secrets management tool. 4. Check if the solution provides auditing capabilities for access to and use of secrets by reviewing logs or audit trails provided by the secrets management tool. 5. Ensure that secrets are encrypted both at rest and in transit by checking the configuration settings of the external secrets management solution. 6. Document your findings and ensure that any gaps in the secrets management process are addressed, potentially by implementing a recommended solution if none is in place.

column4: Create administrative boundaries between resources using namespaces
column5: Use namespaces to isolate your Kubernetes objects.
column6: Limiting the scope of user permissions can reduce the impact of mistakes or malicious activities. A Kubernetes namespace allows you to partition created resources into logically named groups. Resources created in one namespace can be hidden from other namespaces. By default, each resource created by a user in Kubernetes cluster runs in a default namespace, called default . You can create additional namespaces and attach resources and users to them. You can use Kubernetes Authorization plugins to create policies that segregate access to namespace resources between different users.
column7: Run the below command and review the namespaces created in the cluster. kubectl get namespaces Ensure that these namespaces are the ones you need and are adequately administered as per your requirements.
column8: Follow the documentation and create namespaces for objects in your deployment as you need them.
column10: You need to switch between namespaces for administration.
column14: By default, Kubernetes starts with two initial namespaces: 1. default - The default namespace for objects with no other namespace 2. kube-system - The namespace for objects created by the Kubernetes system 3. kube-node-lease - Namespace used for node heartbeats 4. kube-public - Namespace used for public information in a cluster
column19: The task of verifying namespace creation and administration can be effectively automated using Kubernetes CLI commands. The command `kubectl get namespaces` provides a straightforward method to list all namespaces, which aligns with the compliance control's audit steps. While the second opinion suggests additional checks for network policies and resource quotas, the primary requirement is to ensure namespaces are created and managed, which is achievable through automation.

column4: Ensure that the seccomp profile is set to docker/default in your pod definitions
column5: Enable docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions.
column6: Seccomp (secure computing mode) is used to restrict the set of system calls applications can make, allowing cluster administrators greater control over the security of workloads running in the cluster. Kubernetes disables seccomp profiles by default for historical reasons. You should enable it to ensure that the workloads have restricted actions available within the container.
column7: Review the pod definitions in your cluster. It should create a line as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
column8: Use security context to enable the docker/default seccomp profile in your pod definitions. An example is as below: securityContext: seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault
column10: If the docker/default seccomp profile is too restrictive for you, you would have to create/manage your own seccomp profiles.
column14: By default, seccomp profile is set to unconfined which means that no seccomp profiles are enabled.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that the audit of the seccomp profile can be automated using Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl` commands. The seccomp profile is a configuration that can be retrieved from pod specifications, making it feasible to automate the compliance check. The second opinion correctly points out the need to check both pod-level and container-level configurations, which can be addressed with an enhanced command.
column20: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o jsonpath` - Properties to Check: `.spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type` and `.spec.containers[*].securityContext.seccompProfile.type` - Pass Criteria: All pods and their containers have `seccompProfile.type` set to `RuntimeDefault`. - Fail Criteria: Any pod or container has `seccompProfile.type` set to `Unconfined` or is missing the seccomp profile configuration.

column4: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
column5: Apply Security Context to Your Pods and Containers
column6: A security context defines the operating system security settings (uid, gid, capabilities, SELinux role, etc..) applied to a container. When designing your containers and pods, make sure that you configure the security context for your pods, containers, and volumes. A security context is a property defined in the deployment yaml. It controls the security parameters that will be assigned to the pod/container/volume. There are two levels of security context: pod level security context, and container level security context.
column7: Review the pod definitions in your cluster and verify that you have security contexts defined as appropriate.
column8: Follow the Kubernetes documentation and apply security contexts to your pods. For a suggested list of security contexts, you may refer to the CIS Security Benchmark for Docker Containers.
column10: If you incorrectly apply security contexts, you may have trouble running the pods.
column14: By default, no security contexts are automatically applied to pods.
column19: Both AI opinions agree that the audit of security contexts in pod specifications can be automated using Kubernetes APIs or the `kubectl` command-line tool. The task involves checking the presence and correctness of security context fields, which are accessible programmatically. Kubernetes provides the necessary capabilities to automate this compliance check effectively, making manual inspection unnecessary.
column20: - Kubernetes Resource/Service: Pods - API/CLI Method: `kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json` - Properties to Check: `spec.securityContext` at both pod and container levels, including attributes like `runAsUser`, `runAsGroup`, `fsGroup`, `runAsNonRoot`, and capabilities. - Pass Criteria: Each pod and container must have a defined `securityContext` with appropriate settings, such as `runAsNonRoot` set to true and necessary capabilities defined. - Fail Criteria: Absence of `securityContext` or incomplete settings that do not adhere to security best practices.

column4: The default namespace should not be used
column5: Kubernetes provides a default namespace, where objects are placed if no namespace is specified for them. Placing objects in this namespace makes application of RBAC and other controls more difficult.
column6: Resources in a Kubernetes cluster should be segregated by namespace, to allow for security controls to be applied at that level and to make it easier to manage resources.
column7: Run this command to list objects in default namespace kubectl get $(kubectl api-resources --verbs=list --namespaced=true -o name | paste -sd, -) --ignore-not-found -n default The only entries there should be system managed resources such as the kubernetes service
column8: Ensure that namespaces are created to allow for appropriate segregation of Kubernetes resources and that all new resources are created in a specific namespace.
